coredata.dc:title: In-situ fabrication of carbon dots on intense cyan-blue emitting CaAl2O4:Ce3+ phosphor for enhancing thermal sensing, anti-counterfeiting and fingerprint detection applications

coredata.dc:description: In this study, we successfully synthesized CaAl2O4:Ce3+ phosphors using the solution combustion method and (CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+) composites are prepared via the hydrothermal method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) have been performed to confirm crystal structure and phase purity of phosphor and composite. Surface morphology, compositional and functional groups analysis are investigated using FE-SEM, EDXS and FTIR techniques. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis revealed a broad excitation peak at 341 nm and a blue emission peak at 420 nm, associated with the 5d→4f transition of Ce³⁺ ions. The optimal PL intensity is achieved with a Ce³⁺ doping concentration of 7 mol%. Adding 5 wt% carbon dots (CDs) shifted the emission to 432 nm and enhanced the emission intensity by 28.5 times, attributed to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between the CDs and Ce³⁺ ions. The 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite demonstrated cyan-blue emission with Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.130, 0.120), color purity (CP) of 89.5 %, and a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5719 K. The luminescence efficiency of the sample reaches 91 % of the initial intensity at 420 K. Thermal quenching analysis revealed a high activation energy of 0.352 eV, indicating strong thermal stability. The maximum relative sensitivity of 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites 1.07 × 10−2 % K−1 at 300 K. In this study, we utilized the optimized 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites for forensic applications, including latent fingerprint detection and anti-counterfeiting (AC). Latent fingerprints (LFPs) treated with CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphors produced clear images with well-defined level I-III details, while the security ink is visible on various surfaces.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we successfully synthesized CaAl2O4:Ce3+ phosphors using the solution combustion method and (CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+) composites are prepared via the hydrothermal method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) have been performed to confirm crystal structure and phase purity of phosphor and composite. Surface morphology, compositional and functional groups analysis are investigated using FE-SEM, EDXS and FTIR techniques. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis revealed a broad excitation peak at 341 nm and a blue emission peak at 420 nm, associated with the 5d→4f transition of Ce³⁺ ions. The optimal PL intensity is achieved with a Ce³⁺ doping concentration of 7 mol%. Adding 5 wt% carbon dots (CDs) shifted the emission to 432 nm and enhanced the emission intensity by 28.5 times, attributed to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between the CDs and Ce³⁺ ions. The 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite demonstrated cyan-blue emission with Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.130, 0.120), color purity (CP) of 89.5 %, and a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 5719 K. The luminescence efficiency of the sample reaches 91 % of the initial intensity at 420 K. Thermal quenching analysis revealed a high activation energy of 0.352 eV, indicating strong thermal stability. The maximum relative sensitivity of 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites 1.07 × 10−2 % K−1 at 300 K. In this study, we utilized the optimized 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites for forensic applications, including latent fingerprint detection and anti-counterfeiting (AC). Latent fingerprints (LFPs) treated with CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphors produced clear images with well-defined level I-III details, while the security ink is visible on various surfaces.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare-earth (RE) doped materials exhibit distinctive luminescent properties, making them useful in numerous applications, and have garnered significant research interest [1,2]. Alkaline earth silicates, aluminates, and metal sulfides, when doped with RE elements, can absorb energy from their surroundings and convert it into light. Among these, RE ion-activated alkaline earth aluminates stand out due to their high quantum efficiency, stability, and excellent luminescence compared to silicates and metal sulfides. For instance, SrAl2O4:Eu2+, Dy3+ phosphors, which emit yellow-green light, are well-established and widely used industrially [3,4]. CaAl2O4: Eu2+ phosphors have broadband excitation and emission spectra compatible with blue LED chips [5,6]. CaAl2O4 also boasts excellent thermal and chemical stability. Cerium (Ce3+), a highly effective dopant, exhibits broadband emission due to its 5d–4f transitions, which are sensitive to the crystal field. Ce3+ doped CaAl2O4 phosphors are promising for LED applications, and their broad UV absorption depends on the host matrix [7–11]. While some studies have explored Ce3+ doped phosphors like CaSr2Al2O6: Ce3+, Ca2Al2SiO7: Ce3+ and SrZrSi2O7: Ce3+, which all emit blue light, research on efficient Ce3+-doped blue phosphors remains limited [9–11].

RE doped nanomaterials, however, often experience reduced luminescence quantum efficiency due to surface defects, low molar absorptivity, and narrow absorption bands. Enhancing surface properties and quantum efficiency is essential to overcome these limitations. CDs provide an effective solution, serving as sensitizers and surface modifiers to improve PL by tuning the optical band gap of the target materials. CDs are attractive due to their low toxicity, high stability, and strong luminescence, making them valuable in various applications. Integrating CDs into phosphors improves both morphology and luminous efficiency [12,13]. Recent research has demonstrated that CDs, when combined with RE doped materials like YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+, Tb3+/CDs, boost PL through FRET [14,15]. For instance, CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+show stronger PL intensity than CaAl2O4:7Ce3+due to FRET interactions [15].

Fingerprints (FPs) are ridge patterns on the fingertips that have evolved naturally during human evolution. These patterns are not only unique but also heritable and invariant, offering sufficient complexity for personal identification. LFPs, which are invisible marks left at crime scenes, often serve as critical evidence for identifying suspects [16,17]. Traditional methods for detecting LFPs include the ninhydrin method, cyanoacrylate fuming, and powder dusting. The ninhydrin method involves volatile acetone and ninhydrin, which pose health risks, while cyanoacrylate fuming is limited to non-porous surfaces. Among these, the powder dusting method stands out due to its simplicity, safety, and ease of use [18–20]. Compared to conventional powders, such as metallic or magnetic powders, phosphors offer superior contrast, selectivity, and reduced background interference, making them a more effective option [21,22]. Literature survey on comparison of materials, substrates, methods of development, visualization techniques, key fingerprint features (Type I, II, and III), and aging time for latent fingerprint detection studies other doped phosphors with present work is provided in Table 1 [23–32].

The issues of AC and information security have gained significant attention in recent years [33,34]. Security ink has emerged as a prominent technology, capable of creating invisible AC markers, text, images, and other elements to ensure product security and document encryption [35,36]. However, many current security inks incorporate toxic solvents such as hexane, toluene, cadmium selenide, and cadmium sulfide, posing risks to both human health and the environment. Therefore, developing efficient and environmentally safe fluorescent materials for security ink applications is of great importance [37–42]. Consequently, the integration of phosphors with organic solvents has emerged as a promising approach for developing AC inks in the field of security. Comparison of AC materials, emission colors, methods of application, security features, limitations, and aging times of present work with other doped phosphors and composites are summarized in Table 2 [43–52].

Nano thermometers have become increasingly significant in research, technology, healthcare, and commercial sectors, owing to their distinctive spectral characteristics and exceptional chemical and physical stability [53–56]. Among the various options, rare-earth-doped inorganic nanoparticles stand out as a compelling choice for temperature monitoring across a wide temperature range. Frequently employed spectroscopic metrics for luminescence thermometers include temperature stability, thermal activation energy (Ea), relative sensitivity (Sr)emission lifetime, internal quantum efficiency (IQE) [57–60]. Comparison of optical thermometric parameters of other doped phosphors with present work are listed in Table 3 [61–69].

In this work, we synthesized highly stable blue-emitting CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites using a hydrothermal-assisted combustion method at a relatively low temperature (800 °C). These composites can be applied in fields such as optical thermometry, forensic science (for latent fingerprint detection), AC and solid-state lighting. We investigated the structure, morphology, elemental composition, PL properties, temperature, and concentration quenching effects of the composites. Additionally, we examined their CIE coordinates, CP and CCT, with a focus on their applications in temperature sensing and latent fingerprint detection.

The materials utilized in this study include Calcium nitrate hexahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·6H2O) with a purity of 98 %, Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O) at 98.0 % purity, Cerium (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Ce(NO₃)₃·5H2O) with 99.9 % purity, and urea (NH2CONH2) with a purity of 99.5 %. All of these chemicals are sourced from Sigma Aldrich, India. Distilled water used throughout the experiment is prepared in the laboratory.

The synthesis process began by dissolving the required stoichiometric amounts of starting materials in water. This solution is heated to approximately 80 °C, which caused it to thicken into a gel-like form as the water evaporated. Afterward, a measured amount of fuel, along with deionized water, is added to the solution, and it is further heated. The gel mixture underwent combustion for 15 min in a furnace preheated to 400 °C. During this exothermic combustion process, gases such as carbon and nitrogen oxides are released, leading to the formation of nanocrystalline materials at a relatively low temperature. The porous product is cooled and ground into a fine powder, which is subsequently calcined at 800 °C to yield the desired CaAl2O4:Ce3+ phosphors. A schematic of the synthesis of CaAl2O4:Ce3+ (1–11 mol %) phosphor is shown in Fig. 1 a.

For the synthesis of Lemon peel CDs, 100 g of lemons are washed, peeled, and the peel is cut into small pieces, then stored in a freezer at 15 °C. To create the LPCDs, 5 g of the frozen lemon peel is treated with 40 N H3PO4 and 10 mL of distilled water. The mixture is sonicated for 20 min, then heated at 160 °C for 45 min. The resulting dark brown solution is filtered and neutralized using a 1.0 M NaOH solution. The LPCDs obtained are stored at 5 °C for further analysis. A schematic of the synthesis of LPCDs is shown in Fig. 1b.

To prepare the composites, 10 mL of distilled water is combined with 2 mL of LPCDs and stirred for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.05 g of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor, mixed with 3 mL of water, is added to the solution. This mixture is agitated for another 10 min before being transferred into a 100 mL autoclave, where it is allowed to react for 8 h at 200 °C. After cooling, the solution is rinsed with ethanol and centrifuged three times at 4500 rpm. Since the CDs are impregnated on the surface of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+, dialysis is unnecessary. The final product is dried at 80 °C for 6 h. This method enables the large-scale production of the composites efficiently, without requiring lengthy procedures. A schematic of the synthesis of CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ (1–5 wt %) composite is shown in Fig. 1c.

A 26-year-old male volunteer's thumbprint is used to create impressions on various surfaces, including aluminum foil, glass, and paper. After washing and thoroughly drying his hand, the volunteer applied his fingerprint, which is then dusted with CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites using a soft feather brush. Fingerprint images are captured with a 64 MP smartphone under both normal and UV light (365 nm), with a smartphone macro lens used to enhance fingerprint details. Fig. 2 a illustrates the step-by-step process for developing LFPs using the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composite via powder dusting.

Security ink is formulated by dispersing CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites in a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution. To prepare the ink, 10 mL of distilled water is combined with an appropriate amount of PVA, which is then heated to 85 °C while stirring continuously with a magnetic stirrer until the solution became clear. CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are added to the PVA solution using ultrasonic dispersion, resulting in the formation of security ink. This ink is applied to various substrates, including plastic, painted wood, currency, paper, aluminum foil, glass, and metal sheets, using a simple sketch pen or screen-printing technique. The anti-counterfeit patterns produced are photographed under daylight and UV light (365 nm). The schematic representation of creation of AC design via brush and scree-print mode is shown in Fig. 2b.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the synthesized phosphors are examined using a Shimadzu-7000 instrument, with the FULLPROF software employed for crystal structure confirmation. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; JOEL SEM Model JSM 840) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100) are used to capture micrographs and analyze particle sizes. PLE and PL spectra are recorded using a Horiba Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer with a 450 W xenon flash lamp as the excitation source, and all spectra are measured with a 1 nm slit width. The PL lifetimes are measured using an FLS920 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) equipped with a 60 W xenon flash lamp. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements are performed with an ESCALAB Xi spectrometer from Thermo Fischer Scientific. Chromaticity coordinates, CP and CCT are calculated using MATLAB software.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized phosphors are analyzed in detail to determine their crystalline nature and phase identification. Fig. 3 a presents the diffraction spectra for undoped CaAl2O4 and Ce3+-doped CaAl2O4 with varying concentrations (1–11 mol %). All samples show clear crystallization, as confirmed by the diffraction profiles. These profiles align well with the standard ICDD number 53–191 [70] confirming the presence of a monoclinic crystal phase with the P
2
1/n space group [71]. The addition of Ce3+ ions at various concentrations did not introduce any new peaks, indicating the successful and pure synthesis of CaAl2O4:Ce3+ (x = 1–11 mol%) phosphors. However, slight shifts in peak positions are observed in the doped samples, which confirm the incorporation of Ce3+ ions into the crystal lattice. Specifically, the XRD peaks shifted toward lower angles as the dopant concentration increased, attributed to the substitution of smaller Ca2+ ions with larger Ce3+ ions, as shown in Fig. 3b. The ionic radius of Ca2+ is 1 Å (CN = 6), while Ce3+ (r = 1.01 Å for CN = 6) substitutes Ca2+ within the host lattice. The shift is consistent with Bragg's relation (2d sin θ = nλ), which indicates that to maintain a constant nλ, the 2θ angle must decrease, explaining the peak shift toward lower angles [72]. The Ce3+ ions can replace Ca2+ ions because of their similar charges, and two distinct sites (Ca3+ and Al3+) in the host material can accommodate the activator ions. For successful substitution, the ionic radii difference between the host and dopant ions must not exceed 30 % [73] This percentage difference in ionic radii (Dr) between the host and dopant ions is calculated using the following equation [74]:

Where Rh(CN) and Rd(CN) represent the ionic radii of the host and dopant ions, respectively, considering their coordination numbers (CN). The computed Dr value for this study is below 30 %, confirming the successful substitution of Ca2+ by Ce3+ ions. Additionally, the XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs in Fig. 3c reveals a hump at 2θ values of 19.4 ° and 40.7 °, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of amorphous carbon, respectively. For the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite, a hump at 2θ = 19.4 ° and 40.7 ° can be attributed to the presence of CDs, as depicted in Fig. 3d. The incorporation of CDs also enhanced the peak intensities, indicating that redox reactions due to the presence of CDs improved the crystallinity of the host matrix [75,76].

Phosphors experience strain from factors such as crystalline defects, size variations, dislocations, distortions, point defects, grain boundaries, and stacking faults [77]. The Scherrer's equation accounts for the broadening of XRD peaks due to crystallite size but does not consider the effects of strain. The Stokes-Wilson equation, however, connects strain to defects and distortions using the following formula: ε = βhkl/4tanθ [78] This formula, incorporated into the Williamson-Hall (WH) equation, differentiates between peak broadening caused by crystallite size and strain. The total peak broadening (βhkl) is the sum of the size-related broadening (βD) and strain-related broadening (βS) [79]:
(2)
β
h k l
=
β S
+
β D

Strain (βS) can be further expressed as:
(3)
β S
= 4 ε
tan
θ

The WH equation, which combines the Scherrer equation with the Stokes-Wilson equation, is:
(4)
β

=
(

)

By rearranging, we obtain:
(5)

A plot of βhklcosθ against 4sinθ is shown in Fig. 4 a. The slope of this plot indicates the strain in the material, while the reciprocal of the intercept provides the crystallite size. A positive slope signifies lattice expansion due to intrinsic strain [80]. The results of the W-H analysis for the CaAl2O4:Ce3+ phosphors are summarized in Table 4 . The Halder-Wagner (H-W) method operates under the assumption that peak broadening follows a symmetric Voigt function [81,82], which is a convolution of Lorentzian and Gaussian functions [83]. In this method, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak is expressed as:

Fig. 4b illustrates a plot of (β∗hkl/d∗hkl)2 term on the y-axis and (β∗hkl)2/(d∗hkl)2 on the x-axis for each peak.

In this approach, information from high-angle reflections is given less emphasis, as XRD data tend to be of lower quality at higher angles with increased peak overlap, yielding improved results for isotropic broadening [85]. Based on this assumption, the strain profile is modeled by a Gaussian function, while the crystallite size is represented by a Lorentzian function. Furthermore, equation (8) offers an expression for the total broadening associated with this method [86].

In this context, βL and βG represent the peak broadening as determined by the Lorentzian and Gaussian functions, respectively. Equation (9) presents the formula used in the SSP method for calculating crystallite size. The equation applied for measuring crystallite size via the SSP method is as follows:

In this method, the y-axis is represented by (dhkl.βhkl.cosθ)2, and the x-axis by (βhkl.dhkl 2.cosθ) (Fig. 4c). The slope and intercept correspond to kλ/D and (ε/2)2, respectively, providing values for crystallite size and strain. The estimated crystallite size (D) and strain are tabulated in Table 4. Further, Fig. 4d illustrates the relationship between Ce³⁺ concentration and crystallite size for SSP, H-W, W-H and Scherrer's methods. As the Ce³⁺ concentration increases from 1 to 11 mol %, there is a general trend of decreasing grain size across all methods. The SSP and H-W Plot display a relatively steady decline in grain size, while the W-H Plot also shows a similar decreasing trend but with slight fluctuations. The Scherrer's method consistently exhibits the smallest grain sizes compared to the other methods, showing a more pronounced decrease as Ce³⁺ concentration increases. This suggests that higher Ce³⁺ concentrations are associated with a refinement in grain size across different analysis techniques.

Detailed structural data of CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ phosphors can be obtained through Rietveld refinement using the FullProf software [87]. Fig. 5 a–g shows representative Rietveld refinement patterns for un-doped CaAl₂O₄ and samples doped with 1–11 mol% Ce³⁺. The lattice parameters determined from these refinements are listed in Table 5 . For reference, the lattice parameters for a monoclinic crystal structure with the P2₁/n (14) space group are a = 8.70 Å, b = 8.092 Å, c = 15.191 Å, and β = 90.17°. Each XRD profile of CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ exhibits a good fit. The data in Table 5 indicate that the cell volume of CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ increases from 1069.45 to 1077.17 Å³ as the Ce³⁺ doping concentration rises from 1 to 11 mol%. This expansion of the unit cell with increasing Ce³⁺ concentration is evident in the XRD data. In CaAl₂O₄, Ca atoms occupy three unique sites (Ca1, Ca2, and Ca3), and Al atoms are distributed across four distinct sites (Al1, Al2, Al3, and Al4) (Fig. 5h). Both Ca1 and Ca2 are six-fold coordinated with oxygen atoms, forming CaO₆ polyhedra, while Ca3 atoms are coordinated with nine oxygen atoms and share connections with five different AlO₄ tetrahedra [88].

Raman spectra of CDs is shown in Fig. 6 a. The Raman spectrum of CDs typically exhibits two prominent peaks known as the D and G bands. The D band, usually observed around 1379 cm⁻1, is associated with the breathing modes of sp2 carbon atoms in disordered regions. This band indicates the presence of defects, edge sites, or amorphous carbon structures within the CDs. In contrast, the G band, commonly seen around 1596 cm⁻1, corresponds to the in-plane vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms, signifying graphitic domains or ordered graphitic structures. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands, denoted as ID/IG (0.86), provides valuable insights into the degree of disorder within the CDs. A higher ID/IG ratio suggests a greater presence of structural defects or amorphous carbon, while a lower ratio indicates more graphitic or ordered carbon domains. Analyzing these Raman features can help characterize the structural properties and quality of CDs, such as their degree of crystallinity and defect levels. Further, Fig. 6b shows the Raman spectra of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+and 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ exhibit identical peaks between 100 and 1000 cm⁻1, corresponding to the vibrational modes of CaAl2O4. However, in the range between 1350 and 1700 cm⁻1, signals associated with the D and G bands are present only in the 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+sample. The presence of D and G band in 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the composite [89]. The D and G bands are characteristic of carbon materials, with the D band linked to disorder in the carbon structure and the G band representing graphitic carbon, further verifying the presence of CDs in the composite. Where as in CaAl2O4:7Ce3+only the vibrational modes of CaAl2O4 can be observed.

Fig. 6c shows the XPS survey scan spectra of CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite, revealing the elemental composition (Ca, Al, C, O, N, and Ce) and chemical states of the sample. The Ca 2p spectrum shows two distinct peaks at 350.56 eV and 346.43 eV, corresponding to Ca-2p1/2 and Ca-2p3/2, respectively (Fig. 6d), which align with previously reported data [90]. Al 2p region reveals a peak at approximately 74 eV The (Fig. 6e), which is characteristic of aluminum in its oxidized form (Al³⁺). This binding energy confirms the presence of aluminum in the composite material. The 74 eV binding energy is typically associated with Al-O bonds, suggesting that aluminum exists in an oxidized state, likely as part of an oxide or oxyhydroxide compound within the composite. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 6f), three characteristic peaks are identified: C–C/C=C at 284.8 eV, C–O at 286.5 eV, and C=O at 288.3 eV, verifying the presence of CDs. The O 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.3 eV and 534.2 eV, corresponding to C–O–C/C–O–H and C=O, respectively (Fig. 6g). Finally, the Ce 3d spectrum (Fig. 6h) displays a complex structure, with peaks around 887.6 eV and 903.4 eV, which are characteristic of Ce3+ ions [91], confirming the successful doping of cerium into the CaAl2O4 lattice. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 6i) reveals two peaks at 399.6 eV and 400.4 eV, representing pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of un-doped and CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ samples with varying doping concentrations (1–11 mol %) are presented in Fig. 7 a–g. The SEM analysis reveals irregular, non-uniform, micron-sized particles in both the un-doped and doped samples. Notably, even at higher doping levels, the overall morphology of the particles remains consistent, indicating that the incorporation of Ce³⁺ ions do not significantly alter the particle shape or size. This uniformity in morphology across different doping concentrations suggests stable structural characteristics of the CaAl₂O₄ matrix, which is crucial for its potential applications. The SEM analysis shows that the CDs are predominantly spherical and exhibit a relatively uniform size distribution [92], with an average diameter in the nanometer range (Fig. 7h). The surface of the CDs appears smooth, indicating a well-defined structure. As shown in image, the SEM images reveal spherically shaped nanoparticles with distinct edges that corroborate the TEM results. Additionally, the absence of agglomeration suggests good dispersion, which is essential for their applications in sensing and imaging. Further, SEM image of the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor incorporated with CDs illustrate the successful fusion of CDs within the phosphor matrix (Fig. 7i). The SEM analysis reveals that the CDs are uniformly distributed and closely integrated with the phosphor particles, forming a composite structure. Additionally, the SEM images show that the CDs are coated onto the surface of the micron-sized phosphor particles, enhancing the overall morphology [93]. The micron-sized nature of the phosphor particles is attributed to the inherent characteristics of the CaAl₂O₄ matrix during synthesis, which provides a stable framework for the incorporation of nanoscale CDs [94]. This integration is crucial as it not only improves the luminescent properties of the phosphor but also facilitates energy transfer between the CDs and the phosphor matrix, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the composite material for potential applications in optoelectronic devices and phosphor-based systems. The elemental analysis of the CDs shows the presence of carbon and oxygen, confirming their composition (Fig. 7j). In the case of the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite, the elements calcium (Ca), aluminum (Al), cerium (Ce), carbon (C), and oxygen (O) are detected (Fig. 7k). The corresponding elemental mapping further validates the uniform distribution of C and O in CDs (Fig. 7l) and Ca, Al, Ce, C, O elements within the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite material, confirming the successful integration of CDs into the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor matrix (Fig. 7m). This consistent distribution is essential for enhancing the material's functional properties.

The TEM analysis is carried out to investigate the morphological and structural properties of the synthesized CDs. The TEM images (Fig. 8 a) show that the CDs exhibit a well-dispersed distribution of nanoparticles with particle sizes appearing in the range of 5–10 nm. The absence of significant agglomeration in the images indicates good dispersibility of the CDs in the solvent. To obtain a more precise estimation of the particle size, the TEM images are analyzed using ImageJ software [95] (Fig. 8b), revealing that the average particle size of the CDs is approximately 4.6 ± 0.27 nm. This relatively small size suggests that the CDs possess high surface area-to-volume ratios. The narrow size distribution also points toward a uniform synthesis process, providing consistency in the properties of the CDs. Further analysis using high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) is performed to determine the crystallinity of the CDs. The HR-TEM images revealed well-defined lattice fringes, indicating the crystalline nature of the CDs. The measured interplanar spacing for the (002) plane is found to be 0.232 nm (Fig. 8c). This interplanar spacing is consistent with the graphitic structure of carbon. The measured value is well-matched with the line profiles (Fig. 8d) generated using GATAN software [96], providing additional confirmation of the crystallographic properties. The TEM analysis of the optimized CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor is shown in Fig. 8e, which reveals that the particles exhibit agglomerated and relatively non-uniform size distribution, with dimensions ranging from 20 to 50 nm. Also, providing an average particle size of approximately 42.6 nm (Fig. 8f). The narrow distribution of particle sizes suggests a controlled synthesis process, leading to consistent particle growth and minimal agglomeration. HR-TEM images are further utilized to investigate the lattice structure of the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ particles. The interplanar spacing corresponding to the (220) crystal plane is measured to be 0.296 nm (Fig. 8g), which is characteristic of the crystalline phase of CaAl2O4. The measured interplanar distance is well-matched with the line profiles obtained using GATAN software, further validating the crystallographic analysis (Fig. 8h). The presence of the (220) lattice plane suggests that the incorporation of Ce³⁺ ions does not significantly disrupt the crystal structure, allowing the material to maintain its intrinsic properties while potentially enhancing luminescent efficiency through dopant-related effects.

TEM analysis of the CDs incorporated into the CaAl2O4:Ce3+composite provides clear evidence of successful incorporation. The TEM images reveal that the CDs, with an average size of approximately 5.2 nm, are uniformly coated on the surface of the phosphor particles (Fig. 8i and j). This confirms the effective integration of the CDs into the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+matrix, which is crucial for enhancing the composite's overall optical properties. The incorporation of CDs onto the phosphor plays a significant role in improving PL properties through a mechanism known as FRET. In the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite, the close proximity of the CDs to the phosphor surface facilitates energy transfer from the CDs to the phosphor material [97]. The CDs, acting as energy donors, can absorb excitation light and transfer energy non-radiatively to the Ce3+ ions within the phosphor. This FRET mechanism leads to enhanced emission intensity and modified luminescence characteristics, potentially increasing the efficiency of the material for optoelectronic and display applications. Furthermore, the presence of CDs contributes to a broad absorption range, which can enhance the excitation of the phosphor. The nanoscale size and high surface area of the CDs provide additional active sites for interaction with light, thus improving the PL properties. The uniform coating of CDs on the phosphor particles ensures effective energy transfer and minimizes losses, which is beneficial for achieving high-performance luminescent materials. Overall, the TEM analysis confirms the successful incorporation of CDs into the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+composite [98], and the FRET mechanism plays a crucial role in optimizing the PL properties, making this composite material suitable for advanced applications in lighting, sensing, and display technologies. The TEM analysis revealed distinct lattice fringes for both the CDs and the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+phosphor, confirming their crystalline nature. The interplanar spacing for the CDs is measured to be 0.232 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane, which is indicative of the graphitic structure typically found in carbon-based nanomaterials. For the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor, the interplanar spacing is found to be 0.296 nm, associated with the (220) plane, confirming the crystalline phase of the material. These measured interplanar spacings are found to be in good agreement with the line profiles obtained using GATAN software, further validating the crystallographic analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence properties and concentration quenching effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The PLE and PL spectra are presented in the figure. The PLE spectrum displays three distinct bands within the 225–400 nm range (Fig. 9 a). The bands at 275 nm and 341 nm are attributed to the characteristic 4f1 5d0 to 4f0 5d1 transitions of Ce³⁺ ions [99]. The highest peak is observed at approximately 341 nm, corresponding to the lowest energy band in the ground state, which aligns with the emission wavelengths of UV LEDs. When excited by 341 nm light, the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor exhibits a broad asymmetric emission band spanning 350–600 nm, with the most intense peak located at 420 nm. The findings indicate that Ce³⁺ ions occupy different Ca2⁺ sites within the host lattice, and the crystal environment surrounding these ions significantly affects their emission performance. To enhance the luminescence intensity of the phosphors, we synthesized CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ with Ce³⁺ doping concentrations ranging from 1 to 11 mol%, as depicted in the emission spectra shown in Fig. 9b. The emission intensity of the CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ phosphor increases with higher Ce³⁺ doping levels, reaching a peak at 7 mol%, after which it gradually decreases due to concentration quenching effects [100]. The energy transfer between neighboring activators is typically the primary cause of fluorescence quenching. As the Ce³⁺ concentration rises, the average distance between adjacent Ce³⁺ ions decreases, leading to an increase in energy transfer. The energy transfer process between two neighboring Ce³⁺ ions can be illustrated through three mechanisms: radiation reabsorption, exchange interaction, and multipolar interaction. When there is significant spectral overlap between the emission and excitation bands, radiation reabsorption becomes an important factor in energy transfer [101]. However, in the case of CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor, there is minimal overlap between the emission and excitation bands, suggesting that radiation reabsorption can be considered negligible. The significance of multipolar or exchange interactions in energy transfer can be assessed using the typical critical distance (Rc). The exchange interaction is characterized as a short-distance interaction with Rc values typically less than 5 Å [102]. The Rc value for the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ (1–11 mol %) phosphor can be determined using the equation proposed by Blasse [103]:
(10)
R
C =
2
(
3 V
4 π
X C
N
)
1 / 3
In this context, Xc represents the critical concentration of Ce³⁺ ions, N is the total number of host cations in a single unit cell, and V denotes the volume of the unit cell. For the optimized CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor, Xc = 0.07, V = 1075.73, Å3, and N = 3. The calculated Rc is approximately 21.38 Å, which is significantly greater than 5 Å. This indicates that energy transfer is predominantly due to electric multipolar interactions. The mechanisms of electric multipolar interactions include three distinct types: dipole-dipole (d-d) interaction, dipole-quadrupole (d-q) interaction, and quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q) interaction [104]. The specific type of electric multipolar interaction can be determined using the following expression [105]:
(11)
1 x
= K
[
1 + β
( x )
− Q / 3
]
In this expression, x denotes the Ce³⁺ concentration, I represent the luminescence intensity, and K and β are constants specific to the host crystal under identical excitation conditions; Q is a function representing multipolar interactions. When Q takes the values of 6, 8, or 10, the corresponding multipolar interaction types are dipole-dipole (d-d), dipole-quadrupole (d-q), and quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q) interactions, respectively. When the Ce³⁺ concentration exceeds the quenching concentration, Equation (12) can be simplified to the following form [106]:

As illustrated in Fig. 9d, the relationship between log(I/x) and log(x) is approximately linear, with a slope of 1.4319 ± 0.0505. The calculated value of Q is 4.29, which is close to 6; thus, the dipole-dipole (d-d) interaction is the predominant mechanism in the energy conversion process [107].

ce:section.ce:section-title: A comprehensive study on CIE, CP and CCT
ce:section.ce:para:
The CIE coordinates are determined using the Color Calculator Software, while CP is calculated using the following formula [108]:

In this context, (x, y) represents the chromaticity coordinates of the synthesized CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor, (xi, yi) are the CIE coordinates for white light (0.333, 0.333), and (xd, yd) denote the chromaticity coordinates of the dominant color. The chromaticity coordinates of the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor are (x, y) = (0.152, 0.134), indicating a cyan-blue color on the CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram presented in Fig. 9e. The CP of the phosphor is measured at 81 %. The CCT diagram is crucial for estimating the CCT value (Fig. 9f). The CCT values are calculated using McCamy's empirical formula [109]:
(14)

In this context, (x, y) refers to the chromaticity coordinates of the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor, while (xe, ye) represent the epicenter chromaticity coordinates, with values of xe = 0.332 and ye = 0.186. The estimated CIE coordinates and CCT values for CaAl₂O₄:Ce³⁺ (1–11 mol%) phosphors are presented in Table 6 . The CCT of the synthesized CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor is found to be 6788 K. A CCT greater than 4000 K is considered to have a cool light appearance [110]. Therefore, the prepared phosphor demonstrates significant potential for applications in solid-state lighting or cyan-blue lighting for UV-LEDs.

The PL spectrum of the CDs exhibits an excitation peak at 384 nm, leading to an emission peak at 430 nm (Fig. 10 a). This emission in the blue region of the spectrum confirms the formation of blue-emitting CDs. The excitation-dependent PL behavior suggests the presence of surface states or functional groups, which contribute to the blue luminescence. This characteristic makes the CDs promising candidates for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronic devices. The PL analysis of CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ with varying concentrations of CDs specifically 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 wt% shows a significant enhancement in PL intensity as the concentration of CDs increases (Fig. 10b). Notably, the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composite exhibits a remarkable 28.5-fold increase in PL intensity compared to CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ without added CDs. This substantial enhancement in PL intensity can be attributed to the effective energy transfer from the CDs to the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ matrix, demonstrating the role of CDs in improving the luminescent properties of the phosphor. Additionally, the colorimetric properties of CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites with varying concentrations of CDs (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 wt%) are analyzed using CIE coordinates, CP, and CCT values. As shown in the inset of Fig. 10c and d, the estimated CP and CCT values indicate a shift in emission characteristics with increasing CD concentration. The CIE coordinates reveal a progressive enhancement in CP as the concentration of CDs increases, which correlates with an increase in CP values. The CCT values also demonstrate a trend, further supporting the impact of CD concentration on the optical properties of the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites. These findings highlight the tunable emission characteristics of the composite, which are influenced by the addition of CDs. The estimated CP and CCT values are tabulated in Table 6.

The PL intensity of 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite is significantly higher than that of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ due to the FRET between the CDs and Ce3+ ions. FRET typically requires three conditions: proximity between donor and acceptor molecules (usually within 1–10 nm), which has been confirmed through SEM and TEM images (Figs. 7 and 8), showing the CDs closely adhering to the surface of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ spectral overlap between the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption, evidenced by the spectral overlap in Fig. 10e; and parallel dipole orientations of donor and acceptor molecules to facilitate energy transfer via dipole-dipole interactions [111]. Comparative data on the enhanced PL of various CDs composite materials is listed in Table 7 [112–118] demonstrating the 28.5 fold increase in PL intensity for 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ compared to the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor (Fig. 10f). The reduction in FWHM also indicates improved crystallinity due to the inclusion of CDs, which reduces positive and negative defects in the phosphor, leading to an oxidation-reduction effect. In this case, after absorbing light, the CDs (donor) transition to an excited state. From here, they can either radiatively decay via fluorescence or non-radiatively transfer energy to the Ce3+ ions (acceptor). When excited at 384 nm, the CDs facilitate electron transitions within Ce3+ from the 2F5/2 ground state to various excited 5d levels. Simultaneously, the electrons in the CDs transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), absorbing photon energy in the process, as illustrated in Fig. 10g. Through the FRET mechanism, this energy is transferred to the 5D0–2 energy levels of Ce3+, which then relaxes non-radiatively to the 5D0 level before emitting blue light during the transition back to the 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 ground states [119,120]. This results in the significant enhancement of luminescence in the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite. As a result, the composite shows a broad excitation spectrum (260–380 nm) with a primary peak at 384 nm, which aligns with the emission wavelength of commonly used LED chips, making it suitable for a range of luminescent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of phosphors is crucial for their potential applications, especially in high-temperature environments. To assess this, temperature-dependent photoluminescence (TDPL) spectra of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ and 5 wt%CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are recorded between 300K and 500K, as shown in Fig. 11 (a & b). The PL spectra did not show any significant shifts in peak position or shape, but the overall PL intensity decreased as the temperature increased. Fig. 11c and d presents three-dimensional plots of the TDPL spectra for both samples. These plots reveal how PL intensity and emission profile vary with temperature, providing insights into the thermal stability and emission behavior of the materials. As temperature changes, shifts in emission peak positions and intensities are observed, indicating the impact of thermal effects on the luminescent properties. These temperature-dependent PL spectra are crucial for understanding the performance of the samples under different thermal conditions, which is essential for applications in temperature-sensitive environments. The contour plots in Fig. 11e and f illustrate the relationship between temperature and emission spectra for both samples. These plots provide a detailed view of how the emission intensity and peak positions change with increasing temperature.

Fig. 11g and h depicts the variation in the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the characteristic peaks of the CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphor and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composite in the TDPL spectra. The FWHM of the peak at 529 nm increases with rising temperature, attributed to the broadening of the TDPL emission band. This increase in FWHM can be interpreted using the equation [121].
(16)
Γ
( T )
= ħ ω
4 S l n
( 4 )
coth
(
ħ ω
2 k T
)
Here, k is the Boltzmann constant, Г(T) represents the FWHM, ħω denotes the effective phonon energy, and S is known as the Huang-Rhys (H–R) factor. As the temperature increases, lattice vibrations intensify, leading to the thermal quenching observed in the TDPL intensity of the optimized phosphor.

Variations in color intensity across the contour plots highlight shifts in emission profiles and intensity levels as temperature changes, offering a visual representation of thermal quenching effects. At 420 K, the normalized luminescence intensity of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are found to retain 82 % and 91 % of their original intensity, respectively (Fig. 12 a). This stability indicates a promising performance in high-temperature applications such as LEDs. The thermal quenching of PL intensity is mainly influenced by the non-radiative transition probability, which is related to the activation energy (ΔE). A higher ΔE value generally leads to better resistance to thermal quenching. ΔE can be determined using the Arrhenius equation [122]:
(17)
I
( T )
=
I 0
1 + c
exp
(
− E
a
K B
T
)
where I(T) is the integrated PL intensity at temperature T, I0 is the intensity at 300 K, _kB is the Boltzmann constant, and C is a constant. The relationship between ln(I0/I−1) and 1/kBT for the phosphor and the composite is shown in Fig. 12b. The activation energies for CaAl2O4:7Ce3+and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ are approximately 0.287 eV and 0.352 eV, respectively. The higher activation energy (0.352 eV) of the composite indicates superior thermal stability, making it suitable for optical thermometry applications. The plot of ln[FIR] versus 1/T provides critical insights into the thermodynamic behavior of the system under study. In this graph, the natural logarithm of the frequency of an irreversible reaction (FIR) is plotted against the reciprocal of temperature (T) (Fig. 12c). This linear relationship is a manifestation of the Arrhenius equation, which describes how the rate of reaction is influenced by temperature. The stability of emission peaks at elevated temperatures highlights the potential of these materials in LED applications. To ensure precise measurements in optical thermometry, the sensitivity (Sr) is critical. This parameter shows how much the thermometric value changes per Kelvin. The sensitivity can be calculated using the following equation:
(18)

The calculated Sr values for CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ and CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are 1.03 × 10−2 %K−1 and 1.07 × 10−2 %K−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 12d. These results indicate that the composite compares favorably to other luminescent sensors, as listed in Table 8 [123–127]. Additionally, the chromaticity shifts (ΔX and ΔY) for the composite are determined using the following equation [128]:

The calculated chromaticity shifts (ΔX = 4.48 × 10−3 and ΔY = 4.72 × 10−3) demonstrate the superior color stability of the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite (Fig. 12 f). Similarly, for CaAl2O4:7Ce3+, the values are ΔX = 4.87 × 10−3 and ΔY = 4.99 × 10−3 (Fig. 12 e).

The decay behavior of luminescent materials can be modeled using single or double exponential functions, depending on the energy transfer processes involved [129]. For a single exponential decay, the emission intensity I(t) at time t is expressed as:
(20)
I =

)
where I0 is the initial intensity, and τ is the decay time constant. In cases where energy transfer mechanisms are at play, a double exponential function is more appropriate:
(21)

Here, A1 and A2 are constants corresponding to the contribution of each decay component, and τ1 and τ2 represent the time constants of the fast and slow decay components, respectively. The luminescence decay curve of CDs is shown in Fig. 13 a, and the decay curves for CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are depicted in Fig. 13b and c. These curves are best described by a double exponential function rather than a single exponential, indicating the presence of coexistence of intrinsic and surface-state-related emissions, which are commonly observed in luminescent materials. To quantify the average lifetime (τ) of the luminescent materials, the following equation is used [130]:
(22)

Using this equation, the average lifetime of the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors is determined to be approximately 3.051 ms, while the average lifetime for the 5 wt% CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites is slightly longer, at 3.749 ms. The extended lifetime of the composite material indicates an enhanced energy transfer process, which is beneficial for applications that rely on prolonged luminescent performance. Additionally, the lifetime of the CDs alone is about 7.33 ns, reflecting the rapid decay typical of CDs. This double exponential decay behavior is indicative of complex luminescence mechanisms, such as interactions between the dopant ions and the host matrix, making these materials suitable for advanced optical applications where precise control over luminescence timing is crucial.

IQE is a key parameter in evaluating the luminescent performance of materials. The absolute IQE of the synthesized samples is measured using an integrating sphere, following the method described below [131]:
(23)
I
Q E
=
∫
L 1
∫
E 0
− ∫
E 1

Here, L1 represents the integrated area under the emission spectrum, while E1 and E0 correspond to the integrated areas under the excitation band of the sample and the blank (BaSO4), respectively. Using this equation, the IQE values of CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ and the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite are calculated to be approximately 65.74 % and 88.6 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 13d and e.

To assess the practical applicability of the materials in forensic science, LFPs are developed using CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites, as depicted in Fig. 14 a and b. Fingerprint features are classified into three levels. Level I feature represent the overall fingerprint pattern, which can be categorized into loops, arches, and whorls. Level II features include more specific details such as ridge terminations, bifurcations, islands, and spurs, which are essential for automatic fingerprint identification. Level III features, including sweat pores and minutia creases, offer a higher degree of precision for comparative analysis. While both CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors and CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites enable clear visualization of Level I and II features, the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite provides significantly clearer and more detailed Level III features. A comparison of fingerprint extraction developed using CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ phosphors and 5 wt% CDs@CaAl₂O₄:7Ce³⁺ composites reveals a significant enhancement in fingerprint visualization with the addition of CDs. The 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites exhibit intense blue emission under UV illumination, resulting in FPs with higher contrast and clarity. All ridge features are clearly visible, and finer poroscopic details are distinctly observed, making the CDs-enhanced composites more effective for forensic applications. This improvement highlights the role of CDs in enhancing luminescent properties, which contributes to better resolution and detail in fingerprint visualization. Further, pixel profiles of selected region on the FPs developed using phosphors and composites are generated using ImageJ software, the extracted profiles are depicted in Fig. 14c and d. The profiles indicate that the ridge value is higher than the groove value, confirming that phosphor adheres better to the ridges than to the grooves. 3D interactive profiles highlighting the unique phenomenon where composites are stacked on ridges rather than in the furrows (Fig. 14e and f). This innovative representation showcases how the structural characteristics of FPs influence material adherence, revealing that NC demonstrates a stronger affinity for the raised ridge areas.

The composites are also tested on various surfaces, including porous (leaf), semi-porous (aluminum foil), and non-porous (stainless steel) surfaces (Fig. 15 a). Following exposure to a UV light source (365 nm) and subsequent imaging, the FPs on all surfaces are clearly visible, with well-defined ridge and furrow patterns, suggesting strong chemical interaction between the phosphor and the fingerprint residue. Pixel analysis for each surface (Fig. 15b) also demonstrated high contrast between ridges and valleys, underscoring the resolution and clarity of the phosphor's performance in LFP detection across diverse surfaces [132,133] The 3D interactive plots (Fig. 15c) further confirm this interaction for all surface types.

A series of experiments are conducted to evaluate the effects of environmental conditions on the developed FPs. LFPs naturally degrade over time due to water evaporation and lipid breakdown, making it difficult to retrieve clear prints from old samples. In this study, the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composites are used to visualize FPs on aluminum foil over a period ranging from 1 day to 9 weeks. Even after 9 weeks, the fluorescent images of the LFPs showed minimal degradation, as shown in Fig. 16 a, indicating the high stability of the material over time. Photo-stability is tested by subjecting the FPs developed on glass surfaces to continuous UV exposure at 398 nm for up to 50 min. The results, presented in Fig. 16b, show no noticeable deterioration in the fingerprint features, confirming the material's resilience to prolonged UV exposure. Additionally, FPs developed on glass plates are exposed to various temperatures (50 °C-150 °C) for 60 min (Fig. 16c). The results demonstrated that the composites retained clear fingerprint details across this temperature range, highlighting their suitability for use under challenging environmental conditions [134–136]. The phosphors adherence to fingerprint ridges, rather than valleys, is due to their nanoscale size and improved stickiness. Grayscale pixel analysis confirmed this observation, showing a significant contrast in intensity between the ridges and valleys (Fig. 16 d, e, f). The 3D interactive visualizations of the FPs on all tested surfaces (Fig. 16 g, h, i) further confirm the uniform distribution of the phosphors across the ridges, ensuring consistent and reliable fingerprint detection even under varied environmental conditions.

The use of CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors as luminescent inks for AC applications offers an innovative approach to securing documents and items against forgery. Fig. 17 a demonstrates AC markings created on various surfaces, including cardboard, art paper, glass, and OHP sheet, using a simple pen technique with CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ as the luminescent ink. These markings are nearly invisible under natural light, but when exposed to UV light at 365 nm, they emit a distinct blue luminescence, making them clearly visible. This feature underlines the potential of these phosphors for use in secure signatures or personalized security markers that are concealed under normal conditions but easily revealed with UV light. Such invisibility in regular lighting adds an extra layer of security, as it is difficult for counterfeiters to detect or replicate the markings without the specific UV detection method [137,138]. In Fig. 17b, a similar AC application is showcased using screen printing to apply the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite ink onto different substrates. Like the pen-applied markings, these screen-printed AC markings are faint in daylight but become vibrant when exposed to UV light. This technique provides a scalable method for printing anti-counterfeit details on a wide range of materials, making it suitable for large-scale applications, such as packaging, certificates, or secure documents. The consistency of the UV-triggered blue emission, regardless of the substrate, demonstrates the versatility of this composite ink in providing secure, invisible markings across multiple surfaces.

One of the critical aspects of any AC technology is the durability and stability of the security features under different environmental conditions. The resilience of CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphors is tested under various conditions to ensure their practicality in real-world applications. Fig. 18 a highlights the durability of AC markings developed on glass surfaces and subjected to elevated temperatures of 60 °C, 120 °C and 180 °C. Remarkably, the phosphor markings only exhibited a slight reduction in intensity, even at 180 °C, indicating their high thermal stability [139–141]. This resilience to heat ensures that the luminescent ink can withstand harsh conditions, making it suitable for applications where items may be exposed to elevated temperatures during transport or storage. Further, Fig. 18b presents AC markings drawn on art paper and observed under UV light over an extended period of 140 days. The luminescent markings retained their structure and brightness with minimal fading, demonstrating long-term photostability, which is crucial for AC applications where the security features must remain intact for extended periods. Such prolonged durability ensures that the items or documents marked with this ink can be securely tracked and verified over long durations without significant degradation of the visible security features [142,143]. The cyan-blue luminescence emitted from the AC labels is shown to remain consistent over time. Fig. 18c depicts how the emission intensity remains stable for up to 120 min under continuous UV exposure, without notable loss in brightness. This photostability ensures that the AC markings remain visible for extended periods when exposed to UV light, further solidifying the ink's usefulness in AC applications where long-term exposure to light may occur during verification processes. The luminescent quality of the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite ink does show some sensitivity to changes in pH, as illustrated in Fig. 18d. As the pH varies, there is a slight decrease in luminescence, suggesting that while the ink is highly stable, extreme chemical environments could influence its performance. However, under standard conditions, the ink's performance remains robust, making it suitable for a variety of AC applications where pH variations are minimal. This pH sensitivity, combined with the high durability and thermal stability, makes the CDs@ CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite ink an excellent candidate for secure document encryption and other AC uses. The ability to apply the ink in various forms whether through a pen for personalized markings or via screen printing for mass production adds versatility to its application, enabling the creation of secure, tamper-proof items across different industries. These features, including robust stability, prolonged photostability, and effective response under UV light, position this luminescent ink as a promising tool for the development of advanced anti-counterfeit technologies, offering protection for valuable documents, products, and currency against forgery.

In summary, a series of CaAl2O4:Ce3+ (1–11 mol %) phosphors and CDs incorporated phosphor are successfully synthesized using the solution combustion and hydrothermal method. PL analysis of the phosphor revealed a broad excitation peak at 341 nm and an emission peak at 420 nm, corresponding to the 5d→4f allowed transition of Ce3+ ions. The introduction of CDs shifted this emission peak to 432 nm. The emission intensity of the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite is found to be 28.5 times higher than that of the CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ phosphor, without altering the phase structure or morphology. This significant increase in brightness is likely due to the FRET between the CDs and luminescent Ce3+ ions, followed by electron capture by the CDs. The CIE coordinates of the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite are calculated to be (0.130, 0.120), indicating that the composite emits cyan-blue light with a CP of 89.5 % and a CCT of 5719 K. The thermal quenching behavior of the composite demonstrated a high activation energy of 0.352 eV, confirming its excellent thermal stability. Additionally, the composite exhibited superior temperature sensing capabilities in the 300–500 K range, achieving a maximum Sr of 1.07 × 10⁻2 %K⁻1. The LFPs on different substrates are clearly visible, displaying distinct ridge patterns when using the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite. Additionally, the fluorescent-based security ink produced bright and sharp AC patterns under UV light. These results suggest that the 5 wt% CDs@CaAl2O4:7Ce3+ composite is a promising fluorescent material for applications in latent fingerprint visualization and security ink development.
